title: Fitting a water butt to a downpipe
subtitle: Diverter kit, butt, and stand, capturing roof runoff for the garden
excerpt: A 200 litre water butt on a 50 m² roof footprint fills in roughly 2 mm of rainfall. Average UK annual rainfall of 600-1,000 mm means a well-sited butt can supply most summer garden watering needs from a single downpipe. The diverter kit cuts into the downpipe and returns overflow back to the drain when the butt is full.
sourceNotes: Water UK butt installation guidance. WRAP garden water use data. Environment Agency Waterwise water calculator.

recipeTools.notes: For cutting the downpipe at the diverter position.

glossaryTerms.definition: A container (typically 100–500 litres) connected to a downpipe to collect roof runoff for garden use. Most have a lid (to prevent mosquito breeding and debris entry), an overflow pipe, and a tap near the base.

Collecting and storing rainfall for reuse — most commonly roof runoff collected via gutters and downpipes into a butt or underground tank. In the UK, no abstraction licence is required for harvesting from your own roof.

content.text: reduces mains water use for garden watering. A hosepipe uses 12 litres per minute; a 200 L butt holds about 17 minutes of hosepipe use. In a dry summer, a full butt connected to a busy vegetable patch runs out quickly, which is why positioning the butt on the right downpipe (the one receiving the largest roof area) matters.

Choose the downpipe with the largest catchment area (the largest section of roof draining to it). Raise the butt on a stand at least 300 mm high, the tap needs clearance for a watering can below it, and higher elevation gives more pressure at the tap. The overflow outlet must connect back to the drain or disperse to a soakaway.
Step 1, mark and cut the downpipe.

Hold the diverter kit body against the pipe and mark the cut line (usually a horizontal cut 30-50 mm wide). Cut with a hacksaw or utility knife depending on the pipe material (plastic downpipes are soft). Remove the cut section.

Push the diverter body into the gap in the downpipe. The diverter has an internal vane, when the butt is not full, water runs through the vane and out to the butt; when the butt is full, the rising water in the outlet pipe backs up through the vane and diverts back down the main downpipe to the drain. Secure the diverter with the clips or screws provided.
Step 3, connect the outlet pipe and position the butt.
Connect the flexible outlet pipe from the diverter to the butt inlet. Place the butt on its stand directly below. Connect the overflow pipe to a drain or to a second butt in series.

items.symptom: Butt fills very slowly or not at all
items.cause: Diverter vane blocked with debris from the gutter
items.fix: Clean gutters and check the diverter vane is clear. Fit a gutter leaf guard above the downpipe to reduce debris.

items.symptom: Water overflowing at the diverter, not into the butt
items.cause: Outlet pipe kinked or butt tap left open
items.fix: Check the outlet pipe has no tight bends. Close the tap before any significant rainfall.
Fit the lid and ensure it sits flush. Alternatively, place a piece of fine mesh over the inlet.
